title: GitHub's Mysterious robots.txt
summary: Recently, I came across GitHub's robots.txt . There are some interesting entries in there, so I'm going to try and find out why those entries are there. At the time of writing (2023-02-12 14:53 EST), GitHub's robots.txt looks something like this: # If you would like to crawl GitHub contact us via https://support.github.com?tags=dotcom-robots # We also provi…
content:
Recently, I came across GitHub's robots.txt. There are some interesting entries in there, so I'm going to try and find out why those entries are there.

At the time of writing (2023-02-12 14:53 EST), GitHub's robots.txt looks something like this:

# If you would like to crawl GitHub contact us via https://support.github.com?tags=dotcom-robots
# We also provide an extensive API: https://docs.github.com
User-agent: baidu
crawl-delay: 1

User-agent: *

Disallow: /*/pulse
Disallow: /*/tree/
Disallow: /gist/
Disallow: /*/forks
Disallow: /*/stars
Disallow: /*/download
Disallow: /*/revisions
Disallow: /*/issues/new
Disallow: /*/issues/search
Disallow: /*/commits/
Disallow: /*/commits/*?author
Disallow: /*/commits/*?path
Disallow: /*/branches
Disallow: /*/tags
Disallow: /*/contributors
Disallow: /*/comments
Disallow: /*/stargazers
Disallow: /*/archive/
Disallow: /*/blame/
Disallow: /*/watchers
Disallow: /*/network
Disallow: /*/graphs
Disallow: /*/raw/
Disallow: /*/compare/
Disallow: /*/cache/
Disallow: /.git/
Disallow: */.git/
Disallow: /*.git$
Disallow: /search/advanced
Disallow: /search
Disallow: */search
Disallow: /*q=
Disallow: /*.atom$

Disallow: /ekansa/Open-Context-Data
Disallow: /ekansa/opencontext-*
Disallow: */tarball/
Disallow: */zipball/

Disallow: /*source=*
Disallow: /*ref_cta=*
Disallow: /*plan=*
Disallow: /*return_to=*
Disallow: /*ref_loc=*
Disallow: /*setup_organization=*
Disallow: /*source_repo=*
Disallow: /*ref_page=*
Disallow: /*source=*
Disallow: /*referrer=*
Disallow: /*report=*
Disallow: /*author=*
Disallow: /*since=*
Disallow: /*until=*
Disallow: /*commits?author=*
Disallow: /*report-abuse?report=*
Disallow: /*tab=*
Allow: /*?tab=achievements&achievement=*

Disallow: /account-login
Disallow: /Explodingstuff/

The interesting ones to me are:

Disallow: /ekansa/Open-Context-Data
Disallow: /ekansa/opencontext-*
Disallow: /account-login
Disallow: /Explodingstuff/

Why are these repositories and users specifically prevented from being scanned? Let's go through them one by one.

Open Context Data

Finding the reasoning for this one was actually pretty easy since there was an issue on GitHub which led me to a tweet.

Easy enough.

Explodingstuff

This user contains a popular repository with the WannaCry executable. It's fairly clear why they wanted to avoid this site from being indexed, lest GitHub gets flagged as malware.

Account-login

This one is a little more interesting. It's really difficult to search for anything relating to this since "login" and "GitHub" would send me to, well, the login page of GitHub. There are a few places online that I found in relation to account-login, and all of them led me nowhere. Some places I tried: Hacker News comment, Hacker News comment 2, Twitter.

Furthermore, this account doesn't seem to have any form of contact information. Some more strange things show up for this account: here are over 700(!) followers, but account-login is only following one user. This account is very interesting indeed. Why was it blocked? Why does it have so many followers? Why do I start multi-day blog post projects on the Sunday I want to publish them?

To answer those questions, we have to turn to our trusty friend, The Wayback Machine. Looking at the capture information, it seems to be a part of a collection for Wordpress Blogs and the Pages They Link To. Maybe this can tell us something?

Seems like full text searching is disabled and there is no summary data. Maybe we can try and download the archive and try it ourselves? How big can it really be?

Storage_size    472.5 TB (in 2,041,480 files)

Oh. Maybe a new approach is needed. Finding out where this GitHub account was linked from would really help in understanding why it is not indexed. While we're at it, let's contact GitHub support too. It is, however, Sunday evening, and I do want to get this published by the end of the day. Let's do some more digging and try to find any clues.

Let's do a little binary search on the robots.txt through the Wayback Machine and try to estimate when it was added. Many additions to Archive.org's long tail of data reads later, we find that the robots.txt changed to include account-login between 2020-05-13 23:09:28 and 2020-05-13 23:13:24. This coincides with an overhaul of GitHub's robots.txt. Maybe this was added by mistake? We can't rule that out either.

Let's see if there is anywhere else we can use to find backlinks to that account. After checking ahrefs, it seems like all that is linked back to the account are SEO spam/link farm sites (along with a Leetcode account, PyPi project, and a Firefox extension's homepage). Again, there is nothing to point to this account being related to those link-farm websites.

But we still don't know why this domain was blocked from being indexed. Maybe the link farm sites all linking to that specific account raised enough flags that it was blocked? There were barely more than fifteen, and I'm sure that if they were blocking link-farm sites then there would be more than just our good friend account-login.

I am tempted to apply Hanlon's Razor and just chalk this up to a mistake during the migration that nobody bothered to fix, but I feel like there's more to it than that. It might be worth it to sit on publishing this post until I get a response.

[Time skip ooOOoooOoo]

It has now been a few days since I wrote all of that and there has been no response just yet. Maybe I should escalate. Let's go and try to get in touch with a GitHub engineer. Or maybe the CEO. I poke around the official GitHub organization and get in touch with someone over Mastodon. Probably should have done this at the start...

And finally, Martin Woodward gets back to me on Mastodon.

Dug into the PR and looks like the change was introduced because Google was (at the time) putting that poor persons account at the top of the results when someone searched for GitHub. Adding it to our robots.txt fixed that. It's possible (in fact probable) that Google algorithms would be changed now to fix this so we no longer need it, but as it's not doing any harm probably not worth taking it out either.

The change was done as part of some broader SEO optimization work.

That explanation accounts for everything! The high number of followers is because the account was very high up and had a lot of visibility. The weird links from SEO farms could contribute a lot to the account's SEO ranking, which what may have propelled it to the top of the search results.

That was cathartic. It's so nice to know why it was added. Hopefully it helps someone else who else comes across the robots file and tries to find out.

As always, if there is something wrong or you just wanna say hi, feel free to contact me or follow me on Mastodon.
